title: Range type: Type and Value variants
analysis: Task adds Range(Box<Type>) to the Type enum and Range { lower, upper, lower_inclusive, upper_inclusive } to the Value enum in reify-types. The codebase follows a well-established pattern for adding new variants: (1) Type gets a new variant + Display arm + factory method + tests; (2) Value gets a new variant + manual PartialEq arm (using to_bits for f64 fields), Ord arm (with unique type_tag), content_hash arm (with unique tag byte), Display arm, and comprehensive tests. The most recent additions (Orientation, Complex) serve as direct templates. Key implementation decisions: content_hash tag=17 (next after Orientation's 16), Ord type_tag=16 (next after Orientation's 15). Range bounds are Option<Box<Value>> so we must handle None/Some in all trait impls. Display should use interval notation like [lower..upper] with bracket style indicating inclusivity. Both bounds having the same dimension is a type-level concern documented in the task description but enforced outside reify-types (at the type-checker level), since Value itself is untyped at runtime.

steps.description: Write failing tests for Type::Range variant: construction, Display (e.g. 'Range<Int>', 'Range<Scalar[m]>'), factory method, Eq/Hash consistency, not-numeric, as_name returns None
Add Range(Box<Type>) variant to Type enum. Add Display arm: 'Range<{inner}>'. Add factory method Type::range(inner: Type). Verify all Type tests pass

Write failing tests for Value::Range construction and PartialEq: equal ranges are equal, different bounds are unequal, different inclusivity flags are unequal, None vs Some bounds differ, Range != other Value variants

Add Range { lower: Option<Box<Value>>, upper: Option<Box<Value>>, lower_inclusive: bool, upper_inclusive: bool } variant to Value enum. Add PartialEq arm comparing all four fields (bounds via recursive Value PartialEq, bools by ==). Pass PartialEq tests
Write failing tests for Value::Range Ord: cross-type ordering (Range tag=16, after Orientation=15), within-type ordering (lower_inclusive, then lower bound, then upper_inclusive, then upper bound)
Add Ord arm for Value::Range with type_tag=16. Within-type: compare lower_inclusive, then lower (None < Some), then upper_inclusive, then upper (None < Some). Add PartialOrd delegation

Write failing tests for Value::Range content_hash: deterministic (same range = same hash), distinct values produce different hashes, tag=17 doesn't collide with other variant hashes, None vs Some bound differ, inclusivity flag differences produce different hashes

Add content_hash arm for Value::Range with tag=17. Hash structure: tag[1] + lower_inclusive[1] + upper_inclusive[1] + lower_present[1] + optional(lower.content_hash) + upper_present[1] + optional(upper.content_hash). Pass content_hash tests

Write failing tests for Value::Range Display: [lower..upper] for inclusive-inclusive, (lower..upper) for exclusive-exclusive, [lower..upper) and (lower..upper] for mixed, (-inf..upper] for None lower, [lower..inf) for None upper, (-inf..inf) for both None

Add Display arm for Value::Range using interval notation: '[' or '(' for lower bracket based on lower_inclusive, '-inf' for None lower, value Display for Some lower, '..' separator, value Display for Some upper or 'inf' for None, ']' or ')' for upper bracket
Write failing test in tests/exports.rs verifying Type::Range and Value::Range are exported from the crate root and constructible
Update tests/exports.rs with Range variant construction examples for both Type and Value. Verify all integration tests pass including full 'cargo test -p reify-types'

design_decisions.rationale: Tags 0-16 are taken (Bool=0, Int=1, Real=2, String=3, Scalar=4, Undef=5, Enum=6, List=7, Set=8, Map=9, Satisfaction=10, Option=11, Lambda=12, Field=13, Tensor=14, Complex=15, Orientation=16). Next available is 17.
Orientation currently has type_tag=15 (the highest). Range gets 16 to sort after all existing types in cross-type ordering.

design_decisions.decision: Display uses mathematical interval notation: [lower..upper], (lower..upper), etc.
design_decisions.rationale: Interval notation is universally understood for ranges. Brackets [ ] mean inclusive, parens ( ) mean exclusive. Use -inf/inf for unbounded ends. The '..' separator follows Rust range syntax conventions.

design_decisions.decision: Dimension consistency of bounds is NOT enforced at the Value level
design_decisions.rationale: The task says 'checked at type level'. Value is a runtime representation without attached types. The type-checker (in reify-eval or reify-expr) will enforce that both bounds share the same dimension. Adding validation in Value::Range construction would violate the separation of concerns pattern used throughout reify-types.

design_decisions.decision: Ord within Range: lower_inclusive → lower → upper_inclusive → upper
design_decisions.rationale: Follows the pattern of ordering by structural fields left-to-right. Inclusivity flags (bool) are compared first within each bound pair, then the optional values. None sorts before Some (standard Rust Option ordering) which gives unbounded < bounded, a natural semantic.

design_decisions.decision: No factory method on Value for Range (unlike Value::length/Value::angle)
design_decisions.rationale: Range has 4 fields with various combinations. A single factory would need all 4 params, offering no ergonomic benefit over struct literal syntax. Specific convenience constructors (e.g., closed_range, open_range) could be added later if patterns emerge.

reuse.how: Follow the same pattern for Type variant (simple wrapped type), Value variant (struct fields), and all trait impl arms. Orientation's tests serve as direct templates.
Use ContentHash::of() for tag+data hashing, .combine() for chaining recursive hashes of bounds. Same pattern as Option(None/Some) content_hash which also handles presence/absence.
Range bounds are Option<Box<Value>>, same shape as Value::Option's inner. Reuse the comparison approach: delegate to Option's PartialEq/Ord which handles None vs Some correctly.
Follow the naming convention (value_range_*), coverage pattern (construction, eq, ord_cross_type, ord_within_type, display, content_hash_deterministic, content_hash_distinct), and assertion style.
Add Range construction examples following the Orientation pattern: direct Type/Value construction and factory method verification.
